title: St Bene't's Church

infobox.value: The Rt Revd [Dagmar Winter](/wiki/Dagmar_Winter_(bishop) "Dagmar Winter (bishop)"), acting [Bishop of Ely](/wiki/Bishop_of_Ely "Bishop of Ely")

is a [Church of England parish church](/wiki/Church_of_England_parish_church "Church of England parish church") in central [Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge "Cambridge"), England. Parts of the church, most notably the tower, are [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_architecture "Anglo-Saxon architecture"), and it is the oldest church in Cambridgeshire[[1]](#cite_note-Pevsner222-1) as well as the oldest building in Cambridge.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/St_Benets_interior.jpg/500px-St_Benets_interior.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Benets_interior.jpg)

Interior of the nave, looking towards the chancel (right) and north aisle (left)

**St Bene't's Church** is a [Church of England parish church](/wiki/Church_of_England_parish_church "Church of England parish church") in central [Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge "Cambridge"), England. Parts of the church, most notably the tower, are [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_architecture "Anglo-Saxon architecture"), and it is the oldest church in Cambridgeshire[[1]](#cite_note-Pevsner222-1) as well as the oldest building in Cambridge.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The church is dedicated to Saint [Benedict of Nursia](/wiki/Benedict_of_Nursia "Benedict of Nursia"), the founder of the [Benedictine order](/wiki/Benedictine_order "Benedictine order") of monasticism.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Bene't is an attempt to reconcile the Anglo-Norman name [Benet](/wiki/Bennett_(name) "Bennett (name)") with the Latin (and modern English) form of the saint's name Benedict. Latin documents from the 13th and 14th century refer to *"ecclesie sancti Benedicti"* while a contract (in English) of 6 June 1452 for a new roof referred to the "cherche of seynt Bennettys". Lyne's map of Cambridge of 1574 shows "Benett Ch" while Loggan's map of 1688 shows "St Bennetts Church". In the 19th century the church was variously referred to as being of "St Benedict", "St Benet" or "St Bene't". Modern usage is either "St Benet" or "St Bene't".

## Location

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St_Bene%27t%27s_Church&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Location")]

The church is on the south side of [Bene't Street](/wiki/Bene%27t_Street "Bene't Street") next to [Corpus Christi College](/wiki/Corpus_Christi_College,_Cambridge "Corpus Christi College, Cambridge").[[4]](#cite_note-4) St Bene't's was the College's chapel until 1579. The College remains the church's patron, and there are continuing links between the church and the College chapel.[[5]](#cite_note-Roach-5) Opposite the church on the North side of Bene't Street is the [Eagle Pub](/wiki/The_Eagle,_Cambridge "The Eagle, Cambridge").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St_Bene%27t%27s_Church&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

St Bene't's Anglo-Saxon tower was "most probably" built between AD 1000–1050, although the present bell-openings were added in 1586.[[1]](#cite_note-Pevsner222-1)[[6]](#cite_note-EH-6) The tower has [characteristically Anglo-Saxon long-and-short quoins](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_architecture#Diagnostic_features "Anglo-Saxon architecture").[[1]](#cite_note-Pevsner222-1) These project beyond the rubble face, indicating that the tower used to be rendered, as [All Saints' Church, Earls Barton](/wiki/All_Saints%27_Church,_Earls_Barton "All Saints' Church, Earls Barton") in [Northamptonshire](/wiki/Northamptonshire "Northamptonshire") is. Inside the church the 11th-century arch supporting the tower is the most notable feature.[[6]](#cite_note-EH-6)

The arcade of the nave is from a rebuilding of c.1300, when the aisles were also rebuilt. The 14th-century aisles and the north and east walls of the chancel were razed when the church was widened during two [Victorian restorations](/wiki/Victorian_restoration "Victorian restoration"): in 1853 [Raphael Brandon](/wiki/Raphael_Brandon "Raphael Brandon") rebuilt the north aisle and added the porch; in 1872 [Arthur Blomfield](/wiki/Arthur_Blomfield "Arthur Blomfield") rebuilt the south aisle, the chancel and the [clerestory](/wiki/Clerestory "Clerestory") of the nave including the chancel arch. During these works Anglo-Saxon footings of quoins were found which indicated that the original nave was wider than it is today. It is not known when aisles were first added, but it seems likely to have been at a rebuilding before that of c.1300.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In the 13th century the [chancel](/wiki/Chancel "Chancel") was altered, hence the deeply splayed [Early English Gothic](/wiki/English_Gothic_architecture#Early_English_Gothic "English Gothic architecture") [lancet windows](/wiki/Lancet_window "Lancet window") on the south side (one of which is now blocked).[[1]](#cite_note-Pevsner222-1) The [sedilia](/wiki/Sedilia "Sedilia") and [piscina](/wiki/Piscina "Piscina") in the chancel are 14th-century, with [Decorated Gothic](/wiki/English_Gothic_architecture#Decorated_Gothic "English Gothic architecture") [ogeed](/wiki/Ogee "Ogee") arches.[[8]](#cite_note-Pevsner223-8) The [clerestory](/wiki/Clerestory "Clerestory") and roof of the nave are late [Perpendicular Gothic](/wiki/English_Gothic_architecture#Perpendicular_Gothic "English Gothic architecture") and date from 1452.[[6]](#cite_note-EH-6)

St Bene't's has one [monumental brass](/wiki/Monumental_brass "Monumental brass"): a small kneeling figure of [Richard de Billingford](/wiki/Richard_de_Billingford "Richard de Billingford"), who died in 1432[[8]](#cite_note-Pevsner223-8) and had been [Master of Corpus Christi College](/wiki/List_of_Masters_of_Corpus_Christi_College,_Cambridge "List of Masters of Corpus Christi College, Cambridge") from 1398 to 1432.

The church is a [Grade I listed building](/wiki/Listed_building#Categories_of_listed_building "Listed building").[[6]](#cite_note-EH-6)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/St_Benet%27s_Arch_B.jpg/330px-St_Benet%27s_Arch_B.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Benet%27s_Arch_B.jpg)

The 11th-century tower arch with animal carvings

## Bells

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St_Bene%27t%27s_Church&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Bells")]

From its earliest days until the 17th century, the University paid the church to use its bells. A document of 1273 records that the rector agreed to the bells being rung for the University provided the "customary gratification" was paid. This became an annual sum of six shillings and eight pence which was paid as late as 1624, after the tower of [Great St Mary's](/wiki/Church_of_St_Mary_the_Great,_Cambridge "Church of St Mary the Great, Cambridge") had been completed.[[9]](#cite_note-9) In 1655 the University gave thirty shillings "as a free gift" towards the repair of the bells.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The tower has a [ring](/wiki/Change_ringing "Change ringing") of six bells, five of which are 16th or 17th century. Oldest is the second bell, cast by an unknown [bellfounder](/wiki/Bellfounding "Bellfounding") in 1588.[[11]](#cite_note-DoveDetails-11) A local founder, Richard Holdfield of Cambridge,[[12]](#cite_note-DoveFounders-12) cast the third bell in 1607 and the fifth bell in 1610.[[11]](#cite_note-DoveDetails-11) John Draper of [Thetford](/wiki/Thetford "Thetford")[[12]](#cite_note-DoveFounders-12) cast the tenor bell in 1618 and Robert Gurney of [Bury St Edmunds](/wiki/Bury_St_Edmunds "Bury St Edmunds")[[12]](#cite_note-DoveFounders-12) cast the treble bell in 1663.[[11]](#cite_note-DoveDetails-11) The youngest is the fourth bell, cast by William Dobson of [Downham Market](/wiki/Downham_Market "Downham Market")[[12]](#cite_note-DoveFounders-12) in 1825.[[11]](#cite_note-DoveDetails-11) Dobson was a prolific bellfounder and 233 of his bells are known to survive.[[12]](#cite_note-DoveFounders-12) Surviving bells by Holdfield, Draper or Gurney are much rarer.[[12]](#cite_note-DoveFounders-12)

## People

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St_Bene%27t%27s_Church&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: People")]

[Michael Ramsey](/wiki/Michael_Ramsey "Michael Ramsey"), who later became [Archbishop of Canterbury](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Canterbury "Archbishop of Canterbury"), was vicar 1938-1940.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Brothers of the [Society of Saint Francis](/wiki/Society_of_Saint_Francis "Society of Saint Francis") (among them Br [Michael (Fisher)](/wiki/Michael_Fisher_(Anglican_bishop) "Michael Fisher (Anglican bishop)")) served at St Bene't's from 1945 until 2005. The Revd Canon [Angela Tilby](/wiki/Angela_Tilby "Angela Tilby"), a regular contributor to [BBC Radio 4](/wiki/BBC_Radio_4 "BBC Radio 4")'s *[Thought for the Day](/wiki/Thought_for_the_Day "Thought for the Day")*, was vicar, 2007–11. The Revd Canon Anna Matthews, previously precentor of [St Albans Cathedral](/wiki/St_Albans_Cathedral "St Albans Cathedral"), served as vicar from September 2012[[14]](#cite_note-14) until her death in March 2023.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Revd Dr James Gardom, usually Dean of [Pembroke College](/wiki/Pembroke_College,_Cambridge "Pembroke College, Cambridge"), took over the post 2023-24.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The Revd Devin McLachlan has been Vicar since 13 September 2024.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

[Fabian Stedman](/wiki/Fabian_Stedman "Fabian Stedman") (1640–1713), a pioneer in the development of [change ringing](/wiki/Change_ringing "Change ringing"), was clerk of the parish in the mid 17th century.[[5]](#cite_note-Roach-5)

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St_Bene%27t%27s_Church&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: See also")]

* [Leper Chapel of St Mary Magdalene](/wiki/Leper_Chapel,_Cambridge "Leper Chapel, Cambridge"), dating from 1125
* [Church of the Holy Sepulchre, Cambridge](/wiki/Church_of_the_Holy_Sepulchre,_Cambridge "Church of the Holy Sepulchre, Cambridge") or Round Church, dating from 1130
* [School of Pythagoras](/wiki/School_of_Pythagoras "School of Pythagoras"), the oldest secular building in Cambridge, dating from about 1200
